    return results
end

# Experiment with different tolerance levels
tolerance_range = 0.1:0.1:0.8
results = run_schelling_experiment(collect(tolerance_range))

# Plot results
tolerances = [r.tolerance for r in results]
segregations = [r.final_segregation for r in results]
happinesses = [r.final_happiness for r in results]

p1 = plot(tolerances, segregations, marker=:circle, linewidth=2,
         xlabel="Tolerance Threshold", ylabel="Segregation Index",
         title="Segregation vs Tolerance", label="")

p2 = plot(tolerances, happinesses, marker=:


\newpage

# Chapter 7: Evolutionary Dynamics in Structured Populations

The classical evolutionary game theory framework developed in Chapters 2 and 3 assumes well-mixed populations where every individual interacts with every other individual with equal probability. However, real populations exhibit complex spatial and social structures that fundamentally alter evolutionary dynamics. Individuals typically interact more frequently with neighbors, form clusters based on similarity, and experience varying environmental conditions across different locations. This chapter develops the mathematical machinery to analyze evolutionary dynamics in structured populations, revealing how population structure can amplify or suppress natural selection, enable group selection, and facilitate evolutionary innovation through stochastic tunneling.

## 7.1 Evolutionary Dynamics on Graphs

### 7.1.1 The Moran Process on Networks

We begin by extending the Moran process from Chapter 2 to populations with network structure. Consider a finite population of size N arranged on a graph G = (V, E) where vertices represent individuals and edges represent interaction relationships.

> **Definition 7.1 (Graph-Structured Moran Process).** Let G = (V, E) be a connected graph with |V| = N vertices. Each vertex i ∈ V hosts an individual with strategy from finite set S. The fitness of individual i is f_i = Σ_{j∈N(i)} π(s_i, s_j), where N(i) denotes the neighbors of vertex i and π is the payoff matrix. At each time step:
> 1. Select individual i for reproduction with probability proportional to f_i
> 2. Select neighbor j ∈ N(i) for replacement uniformly at random
> 3. Individual j adopts strategy s_i

This process preserves population size while allowing strategy frequencies to evolve through local interactions and replacement.

The key insight is that graph structure creates correlation between interacting partners and replacement targets. On a complete graph (all-to-all connections), this reduces to the classical well-mixed Moran process. However, sparse graphs with local clustering exhibit dramatically different dynamics.

### 7.1.2 Fixation Probability Analysis

The central quantity of interest is the fixation probability ρ - the probability that a single mutant of type A introduced into a population of type B individuals eventually takes over the entire population.

> **Definition 7.2 (Fixation Probability).** For a graph G with fitness parameters r_A and r_B, let ρ_i(G) denote the probability that a single A-mutant initially placed at vertex i eventually fixates (reaches frequency N). The overall fixation probability is ρ(G) = (1/N) Σ_i ρ_i(G).

For the well-mixed case (complete graph), the classical result gives:

ρ(K_N) = (1 - r^{-1})/(1 - r^{-N})

where r = r_A/r_B is the relative fitness ratio. When r > 1 (A fitter than B), ρ > 1/N, indicating positive selection. When r = 1 (neutral evolution), ρ = 1/N.

The fundamental question is: how does graph structure modify this baseline fixation probability?

> **Theorem 7.1 (Isothermal Theorem).** For birth-death updating on any graph G, the fixation probability of a neutral mutant (r = 1) is exactly ρ(G) = 1/N, independent of graph structure.

*Proof.* Under neutral evolution with birth-death updating, the process becomes a symmetric random walk on the state space of mutant configurations. By symmetry, each of the N vertices has equal probability 1/N of hosting the eventual fixation lineage. □

This remarkable result, proven by Lieberman et al. (2005), shows that graph structure affects selection but not neutral drift. However, the dynamics under selection depend critically on graph topology.

### 7.1.3 Amplifiers and Suppressors of Selection

Graph structure can either amplify or suppress the effects of natural selection relative to the well-mixed case.

> **Definition 7.3 (Amplifiers and Suppressors).** A graph G is an:
> - **Amplifier of selection** if ρ(G) > ρ(K_N) for all r ≠ 1
> - **Suppressor of selection** if ρ(G) < ρ(K_N) for all r ≠ 1  
> - **Neutral** if ρ(G) = ρ(K_N) for all r

The star graph provides the most dramatic example of an amplifier:

> **Theorem 7.2 (Star Graph Amplification).** For the star graph S_N with birth-death updating, the fixation probability of a mutant starting at the center is ρ_center = (1-r^{-1})/(1-r^{-(N-1)}) ≈ 1-r^{-(N-1)} for large N and r > 1.

This represents exponential amplification compared to the well-mixed case. Intuitively, a beneficial mutant at the star center directly competes with all other individuals, rapidly spreading its advantage.

Conversely, cycle graphs can suppress selection:

> **Theorem 7.3 (Cycle Suppression).** For large cycles C_N, beneficial mutations spread more slowly than in well-mixed populations, with fixation probability approaching the neutral value 1/N as the cycle length increases.

### 7.1.4 Birth-Death vs Death-Birth Updating

The order of birth and death events fundamentally affects evolutionary dynamics on graphs.

> **Definition 7.4 (Death-Birth Process).** At each time step:
> 1. Select individual i for death uniformly at random
> 2. Each neighbor j ∈ N(i) competes for the vacant site with probability proportional to fitness f_j

Under death-birth updating, the isothermal theorem breaks down - neutral fixation probabilities depend on graph structure. This occurs because death-birth creates fitness-independent vacancies that are then filled through local competition.

```julia
using Graphs, Random, StatsBase

"""
Simulate Moran process on graph with birth-death or death-birth updating
"""
function moran_on_graph(g::AbstractGraph, fitness::Vector{Float64}, 
                        initial_mutants::Vector{Int}, 
                        max_steps::Int = 10000,
                        update_rule::Symbol = :birth_death)
    N = nv(g)
    strategy = zeros(Int, N)  # 0 = wildtype, 1 = mutant
    strategy[initial_mutants] .= 1
    
    for step in 1:max_steps
        if sum(strategy) == 0 || sum(strategy) == N
            break  # Fixation or extinction
        end
        
        if update_rule == :birth_death
            # Birth-death: select reproducer by fitness, then random neighbor dies
            birth_probs = [strategy[i] == 1 ? fitness[1] : fitness[2] for i in 1:N]
            reproducer = sample(1:N, Weights(birth_probs))
            neighbors_list = neighbors(g, reproducer)
            if !isempty(neighbors_list)
                death_target = sample(neighbors_list)
                strategy[death_target] = strategy[reproducer]
            end
        else  # death_birth
            # Death-birth: random death, then neighbors compete for vacant site
            death_target = rand(1:N)
            neighbors_list = neighbors(g, death_target)
            if !isempty(neighbors_list)
                neighbor_fitness = [strategy[i] == 1 ? fitness[1] : fitness[2] 
                                  for i in neighbors_list]
                winner = sample(neighbors_list, Weights(neighbor_fitness))
                strategy[death_target] = strategy[winner]
            end
        end
    end
    
    return sum(strategy) == N  # True if mutants fixated
end

"""
Estimate fixation probability for single mutant on graph
"""
function fixation_probability(g::AbstractGraph, fitness::Vector{Float64}, 
                            trials::Int = 1000, update_rule::Symbol = :birth_death)
    N = nv(g)
    fixations = 0
    
    for trial in 1:trials
        initial_mutant = rand(1:N)
        if moran_on_graph(g, fitness, [initial_mutant], 10000, update_rule)
            fixations += 1
        end
    end
    
    return fixations / trials
end

# Example: Compare fixation on complete graph vs cycle
Random.seed!(123)
N = 20
complete_graph = complete_graph(N)
cycle_graph = cycle_graph(N)

# Fitness values: [mutant_fitness, wildtype_fitness]
fitness_neutral = [1.0, 1.0]
fitness_beneficial = [1.1, 1.0]

println("Neutral evolution (r = 1.0):")
println("Complete graph: ", fixation_probability(complete_graph, fitness_neutral))
println("Cycle graph: ", fixation_probability(cycle_graph, fitness_neutral))
println("Expected (1/N): ", 1/N)

println("\nBeneficial mutant (r = 1.1):")
println("Complete graph: ", fixation_probability(complete_graph, fitness_beneficial))
println("Cycle graph: ", fixation_probability(cycle_graph, fitness_beneficial))

4.5 7.2 Subdivided Populations and Migration

4.5.1 7.2.1 Island Models and Local Adaptation

Many populations exist as subdivided metapopulations connected by migration. The island model treats the population as discrete subpopulations (demes) connected by migration at rate m per generation.

Definition 7.5 (Island Model). Consider k subpopulations each of size n, with total population N = kn. Let x_i(t) denote the frequency of strategy A in subpopulation i. The dynamics are: dx_i/dt = f_A(x_i) - f̄(x_i) + m(x̄ - x_i) where x̄ = (1/k)Σ_j x_j is the global frequency and f_A, f̄ are local fitness functions.

Migration homogenizes frequencies across subpopulations, while local selection creates differentiation. The balance between these forces determines whether populations can maintain local adaptations to different environments.

4.5.2 7.2.2 Wright’s F-Statistics and Population Structure

Population subdivision creates correlation structure that can be quantified using Wright’s F-statistics.

Definition 7.6 (F-Statistics). For a subdivided population: - F_IS measures inbreeding within subpopulations - F_ST measures differentiation between subpopulations
- F_IT measures total inbreeding: (1 - F_IT) = (1 - F_IS)(1 - F_ST)

The key parameter F_ST quantifies population structure:

F_ST = Var(x_i)/[x̄(1 - x̄)]

where x_i is the frequency in subpopulation i and x̄ is the global frequency.

Under the island model with migration rate m, the equilibrium differentiation is:

F_ST = 1/(1 + 4nm)

This shows that population structure (F_ST > 0) requires either small subpopulation size (small n) or limited migration (small m).

4.5.3 7.2.3 Multi-Level Selection

Population structure enables group selection - differential survival or reproduction of entire groups based on their composition.

Definition 7.7 (Multi-Level Selection). The total evolutionary change combines within-group and between-group components: Δx̄ = Δx̄_within + Δx̄_between where Δx̄_within reflects individual selection within groups and Δx̄_between reflects differential group success.

Following Price’s equation, we can decompose this as:

Δx̄ = Cov(x_i, w_i)/w̄ + E[Δx_i]

where x_i is the trait frequency in group i, w_i is group fitness, w̄ is mean group fitness, and E[Δx_i] is the average within-group change.

Group selection can favor traits that are individually costly but group-beneficial, such as cooperation. However, migration undermines group selection by homogenizing group compositions.

Theorem 7.4 (Cooperation in Subdivided Populations). Consider a two-strategy game where cooperators pay cost c to provide benefit b to group members. In a subdivided population with F_ST structure, cooperation can evolve if: b/c > 1/F_ST

This generalizes Hamilton’s rule rb > c to structured populations, where relatedness r is replaced by F_ST.

using DifferentialEquations, Plots

"""
Island model dynamics with local selection and migration
"""
function island_model!(dx, x, p, t)
    n_islands, selection_strength, migration_rate, payoff_matrix = p
    global_freq = mean(x)
    
    for i in 1:n_islands
        # Local selection
        fitness_A = payoff_matrix[1,1] * x[i] + payoff_matrix[1,2] * (1 - x[i])
        fitness_B = payoff_matrix[2,1] * x[i] + payoff_matrix[2,2] * (1 - x[i])
        mean_fitness = x[i] * fitness_A + (1 - x[i]) * fitness_B
        
        selection_term = selection_strength * x[i] * (1 - x[i]) * (fitness_A - fitness_B) / mean_fitness
        
        # Migration
        migration_term = migration_rate * (global_freq - x[i])
        
        dx[i] = selection_term + migration_term
    end
end

# Simulate prisoner's dilemma in subdivided population
n_islands = 10
payoff_matrix = [3.0 0.0; 5.0 1.0]  # T=5, R=3, P=1, S=0
selection_strength = 0.1
migration_rates = [0.01, 0.05, 0.1]

tspan = (0.0, 100.0)
initial_freqs = rand(n_islands) * 0.5  # Random initial frequencies

plt = plot(title="Cooperation in Subdivided Population", 
          xlabel="Time", ylabel="Cooperation Frequency",
          legend=:right)

for m in migration_rates
    p = (n_islands, selection_strength, m, payoff_matrix)
    prob = ODEProblem(island_model!, initial_freqs, tspan, p)
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5())
    
    # Plot mean frequency over time
    mean_freqs = [mean(sol.u[i]) for i in 1:length(sol.t)]
    plot!(plt, sol.t, mean_freqs, label="m = $m", linewidth=2)
    
    # Calculate F_ST at equilibrium
    final_freqs = sol.u[end]
    global_freq = mean(final_freqs)
    if global_freq > 0 && global_freq < 1
        fst = var(final_freqs) / (global_freq * (1 - global_freq))
        println("Migration rate $m: F_ST = $(round(fst, digits=3))")
    end
end

display(plt)

4.6 7.3 Stochastic Tunneling and Fitness Landscapes

4.6.1 7.3.1 Rugged Fitness Landscapes

Real evolutionary dynamics often occur on rugged fitness landscapes with multiple peaks separated by fitness valleys. Populations can become trapped on local optima, unable to reach higher fitness peaks through gradual selection alone.

Definition 7.8 (Fitness Landscape). A fitness landscape is a mapping W: S^N → R from the space of population configurations to fitness values. Local optima are configurations where all single-mutation neighbors have lower fitness.

The key challenge is evolutionary transitions between fitness peaks - processes that require populations to traverse low-fitness regions. In infinite populations, deterministic selection prevents such transitions. However, finite populations can undergo stochastic tunneling through genetic drift.

4.6.2 7.3.2 Tunneling Dynamics

Consider a simple two-peak landscape where populations must pass through an intermediate low-fitness state to reach the global optimum.

Definition 7.9 (Tunneling Time). The tunneling time τ is the expected time for a population starting at a local optimum to reach the global optimum, conditional on making the transition before fixation at the local optimum.

For weak selection (selection strength s << 1) and large populations, the tunneling rate follows:

Rate ∝ exp(-N Δf/s)

where Δf is the fitness cost of the intermediate state and N is the effective population size. This exponential dependence on population size means that tunneling is extremely rare in large populations but can be significant in small populations or those with strong population structure.

4.6.3 7.3.3 Population Structure and Tunneling

Population subdivision can dramatically facilitate stochastic tunneling by reducing the effective population size within subpopulations while maintaining sufficient total population size for evolutionary innovation.

Consider a subdivided population with k subpopulations of size n each. The probability that at least one subpopulation successfully tunnels is approximately:

P_tunnel ≈ 1 - [1 - P_single]^k

where P_single ∝ exp(-n Δf/s) is the single-subpopulation tunneling probability.

This creates an optimal intermediate level of population structure that maximizes evolutionary innovation by balancing within-group tunneling capability with between-group diversity.

using Random, Distributions

"""
Simulate stochastic tunneling in Wright-Fisher population
Fitness landscape: f(0) = 1.0, f(0.5) = 0.8, f(1.0) = 1.2
"""
function wright_fisher_tunneling(N::Int, selection_strength::Float64, 
                                max_generations::Int = 10000)
    
    # Fitness function: two peaks with valley at 0.5
    function fitness(freq)
        if freq < 0.5
            return 1.0 + selection_strength * (0.5 - freq)  # Slope down to valley
        else
            return 0.8 + selection_strength * 2.4 * (freq - 0.5)  # Slope up to global peak
        end
    end
    
    frequency = 0.01  # Start near local optimum at 0
    trajectory = Float64[frequency]
    
    for gen in 1:max_generations
        if frequency >= 0.99  # Reached global optimum
            return (true, trajectory)
        end
        if frequency <= 0.01  # Extinction or return to local optimum
            frequency = 0.01
        end
        
        # Wright-Fisher sampling with selection
        mean_fitness = fitness(frequency)
        expected_count = N * frequency * fitness(frequency) / mean_fitness
        new_count = rand(Binomial(N, expected_count / N))
        frequency = new_count / N
        
        push!(trajectory, frequency)
    end
    
    return (false, trajectory)
end

"""
Compare tunneling success in well-mixed vs subdivided populations
"""
function compare_tunneling_architectures()
    N_total = 200
    selection_strength = 0.1
    trials = 1000
    
    # Well-mixed population
    wellmixed_success = 0
    for trial in 1:trials
        success, _ = wright_fisher_tunneling(N_total, selection_strength)
        if success
            wellmixed_success += 1
        end
    end
    
    # Subdivided population (4 subpopulations of size 50)
    subdivided_success = 0
    n_subpops = 4
    subpop_size = N_total ÷ n_subpops
    
    for trial in 1:trials
        any_success = false
        for subpop in 1:n_subpops
            success, _ = wright_fisher_tunneling(subpop_size, selection_strength)
            if success
                any_success = true
                break
            end
        end
        if any_success
            subdivided_success += 1
        end
    end
    
    println("Tunneling success rates:")
    println("Well-mixed (N=$N_total): $(wellmixed_success/trials)")
    println("Subdivided (4×$(subpop_size)): $(subdivided_success/trials)")
    
    return wellmixed_success/trials, subdivided_success/trials
end

Random.seed!(456)
compare_tunneling_architectures()

4.7 7.4 Adaptive Dynamics

4.7.1 7.4.1 The Canonical Equation of Adaptive Dynamics

Adaptive dynamics provides a deterministic approximation for evolutionary change in continuous trait spaces under weak mutations and strong selection.

Definition 7.10 (Adaptive Dynamics). Consider a population with continuous trait x evolving under selection. The canonical equation of adaptive dynamics is: dx/dt = μ σ²(x) ∂W(y,x)/∂y |_{y=x} where μ is the mutation rate, σ²(x) is the mutational variance, and W(y,x) is the invasion fitness of mutant y in resident population x.

The invasion fitness W(y,x) determines whether a rare mutant with trait y can invade a resident population with trait x. Evolution proceeds in the direction of the selection gradient ∂W(y,x)/∂y |_{y=x}.

4.7.2 7.4.2 Evolutionary Singularities and Stability

Critical points of the adaptive dynamics occur where the selection gradient vanishes.

Definition 7.11 (Evolutionary Singularity). A trait value x* is an evolutionary singularity if ∂W(y,x)/∂y |_{y=x=x*} = 0.

The evolutionary behavior near singularities depends on the second derivatives:

Definition 7.12 (Singularity Classification). An evolutionary singularity x* is: - Evolutionarily stable (ESS) if ∂²W(y,x)/∂y² |{y=x} < 0 - Convergence stable if ∂²W(y,x)/∂y∂x |{y=x} < 0
- A branching point if ESS but not convergence stable

This classification yields four types of singularities: 1. Continuously stable: ESS and convergence stable - evolutionary attractor 2. Garden of Eden: ESS but not convergence stable - unstable optimum
3. Evolutionary repellor: Not ESS but convergence stable - evolutionary saddle 4. Branching point: Convergence stable but not ESS - leads to evolutionary branching

4.7.3 7.4.3 Evolutionary Branching

At branching points, initially monomorphic populations can split into multiple coexisting phenotypes through disruptive selection.

Theorem 7.5 (Evolutionary Branching Condition). A singularity x* undergoes evolutionary branching if: 1. ∂W(y,x)/∂y |{y=x=x} = 0 (singularity condition) 2. ∂²W(y,x)/∂y∂x |{y=x} < 0 (convergence stability)
3. ∂²W(y,x)/∂y² |_{y=x*} > 0 (evolutionary instability)

After branching, the population evolves as a dimorphism with traits x₁(t) and x₂(t) governed by:

dx₁/dt = μ₁ σ₁²(x₁) ∂W(y,x₁,x₂)/∂y |{y=x₁} dx₂/dt = μ₂ σ₂²(x₂) ∂W(y,x₁,x₂)/∂y |{y=x₂}

where W(y,x₁,x₂) is the invasion fitness in the dimorphic resident population.

4.7.4 7.4.4 Example: Resource Competition Model

Consider a population exploiting a continuous resource distribution. Individuals with trait x compete most strongly with others having similar traits.

The invasion fitness for this model is:

W(y,x) = ∫ K(z) C(y,z) dz - ∫ C(y,z) C(x,z) dz

where K(z) is the resource distribution and C(x,z) represents resource utilization efficiency.

For Gaussian utilization functions C(x,z) = exp(-(x-z)²/2σ_c²) and resource distribution K(z) = exp(-z²/2σ_K²), the analysis reveals:

  • If σ_c > σ_K (wide utilization, narrow resources): evolution toward generalism
  • If σ_c < σ_K (narrow utilization, wide resources): evolutionary branching occurs
using DifferentialEquations, Plots

"""
Adaptive dynamics for resource competition model
"""
function resource_competition_dynamics!(dx, x, p, t)
    σ_c, σ_K, μ, σ_mut = p
    
    # Selection gradient for Gaussian competition kernel
    function selection_gradient(trait)
        # Simplified gradient for symmetric Gaussian kernels
        if σ_c < σ_K  # Branching regime
            return -trait  # Directional selection toward x=0
        else  # Stabilizing regime
            return -trait * (1 - σ_c²/σ_K²)
        end
    end
    
    if length(x) == 1  # Monomorphic population
        dx[1] = μ * σ_mut^2 * selection_gradient(x[1])
        
        # Check for branching condition at x=0
        if abs(x[1]) < 0.01 && σ_c < σ_K
            # Initialize branching (in practice, would require stochastic implementation)
            # This is a simplification for demonstration
        end
    else  # Dimorphic population (post-branching)
        # Simplified dimorphic dynamics
        dx[1] = μ * σ_mut^2 * (-x[1] * (1 + (x[1] - x[2])^2))
        dx[2] = μ * σ_mut^2 * (-x[2] * (1 + (x[2] - x[1])^2))
    end
end

"""
Simulate evolutionary branching in resource competition
"""
function simulate_branching()
    # Parameters
    σ_K = 1.0      # Resource distribution width
    σ_c = 0.5      # Competition kernel width (σ_c < σ_K for branching)
    μ = 0.01       # Mutation rate
    σ_mut = 0.1    # Mutational variance
    
    p = (σ_c, σ_K, μ, σ_mut)
    
    # Phase 1: Approach to branching point
    x0 = [2.0]  # Initial trait value
    tspan = (0.0, 100.0)
    prob = ODEProblem(resource_competition_dynamics!, x0, tspan, p)
    sol1 = solve(prob, Tsit5())
    
    # Phase 2: Post-branching dimorphic evolution (simplified)
    x0_dimorphic = [0.1, -0.1]  # Small deviation from branching point
    tspan2 = (0.0, 200.0)
    
    function dimorphic_dynamics!(dx, x, p, t)
        σ_c, σ_K, μ, σ_mut = p
        # Mutual invasibility analysis shows divergence
        dx[1] = μ * σ_mut^2 * 0.5 * x[1] * (1 - x[1]^2/σ_K^2)
        dx[2] = μ * σ_mut^2 * 0.5 * x[2] * (1 - x[2]^2/σ_K^2)
    end
    
    prob2 = ODEProblem(dimorphic_dynamics!, x0_dimorphic, tspan2, p)
    sol2 = solve(prob2, Tsit5())
    
    # Plot results
    p1 = plot(sol1.t, sol1[1,:], label="Monomorphic phase", 
              xlabel="Time", ylabel="Trait value", title="Evolutionary Branching",
              linewidth=2)
    
    p2 = plot(sol2.t, sol2[1,:], label="Branch 1", linewidth=2,
              xlabel="Time", ylabel="Trait value", title="Post-branching Dimorphism")
    plot!(p2, sol2.t, sol2[2,:], label="Branch 2", linewidth=2)
    
    return plot(p1, p2, layout=(1,2), size=(800,300))
end

display(simulate_branching())

4.8 7.5 Applications and Extensions

4.8.1 7.5.1 Eco-evolutionary Dynamics

The integration of ecological and evolutionary timescales requires considering how evolutionary changes in traits affect population dynamics and vice versa. Population structure plays a crucial role in these eco-evolutionary feedbacks.

Consider a predator-prey system where prey evolve anti-predator defenses. The population dynamics are:

dN/dt = rN(1 - N/K(x)) - aNP dP/dt = eaNP - mP

where the carrying capacity K(x) depends on the evolving trait x (defense level). The trait evolution follows adaptive dynamics:

dx/dt = μσ²(x) ∂W(y,x,N,P)/∂y |_{y=x}

This coupling can lead to complex dynamics including evolutionary cycles, where predator pressure drives defensive evolution, reducing prey vulnerability and predator success, eventually relaxing selection for defense.

4.8.2 7.5.2 Cultural Evolution and Social Learning

The mathematical framework developed for genetic evolution extends naturally to cultural evolution, where individuals learn strategies through social transmission rather than genetic inheritance.

Cultural evolution often exhibits different patterns than genetic evolution due to: - Directed variation: Cultural mutations can be biased toward improvement - Horizontal transmission: Individuals can learn from non-relatives
- Multiple inheritance: Individuals can learn from multiple sources - Rapid change: Cultural evolution can be much faster than genetic evolution

Population structure affects cultural evolution through network effects in social learning, similar to how it affects genetic evolution through reproductive networks.

4.9 7.6 Exercises

Exercise 7.1 (Computational). Implement a simulation of the Moran process on different graph structures (complete graph, cycle, star, grid). Compare fixation probabilities for neutral and beneficial mutations. Verify the isothermal theorem for neutral evolution.

Exercise 7.2 (Theoretical). Prove that the star graph is an amplifier of selection by deriving the exact fixation probability formula for a beneficial mutant starting at the center vertex.

Exercise 7.3 (Computational). Simulate the island model with different migration rates and local selection pressures. Plot the relationship between F_ST and migration rate, and verify the theoretical prediction F_ST = 1/(1 + 4nm).

Exercise 7.4 (Theoretical). For a two-island model with different local optima, derive conditions under which migration prevents local adaptation versus facilitates gene flow between adapted populations.

Exercise 7.5 (Computational). Implement Wright-Fisher population dynamics on a rugged fitness landscape. Compare tunneling success rates between well-mixed and subdivided populations of the same total size.

Exercise 7.6 (Theoretical). Show that evolutionary branching requires the singularity to be convergence stable but evolutionarily unstable. Explain why both conditions are necessary.

Exercise 7.7 (Computational). Implement the adaptive dynamics canonical equation for the resource competition model. Verify that branching occurs when σ_c < σ_K and convergent evolution occurs when σ_c > σ_K.

Exercise 7.8 (Theoretical). Derive the invasion fitness W(y,x) for the resource competition model with Gaussian utilization functions and resource distribution. Show that the selection gradient vanishes at x = 0.

Exercise 7.9 (Computational). Simulate a predator-prey eco-evolutionary model where prey defense evolution affects population dynamics. Explore parameter regimes that lead to evolutionary cycles.

Exercise 7.10 (Theoretical). Extend the multi-level selection framework to three levels (individuals, groups, metapopulation). Derive conditions for the evolution of traits that are costly to individuals and groups but beneficial to the metapopulation.

4.10 Connections

This chapter bridges the gap between the abstract mathematical theory of evolutionary games and the complex realities of structured biological and social populations. The insights developed here connect to several other areas of the curriculum:

The network-based models link directly to the graph-theoretic approaches in ABM-001 (Agent-Based Modeling Foundations), while the stochastic processes extend the deterministic game dynamics from Chapter 2. The adaptive dynamics framework provides mathematical tools that will prove essential for the ecological and economic applications explored in

5 Chapter 8: Applications: Ecology, Economics, and Social Systems

The theoretical foundations developed in Chapters 1-7 find their most compelling expression in real-world applications spanning ecology, economics, and social systems. This chapter demonstrates how evolutionary game theory and agent-based modeling illuminate the emergence of complex behaviors in these domains, from the coevolutionary arms races between predators and prey to the spontaneous formation of social norms and the dynamics of economic inequality.

5.1 8.1 Ecological Applications

5.1.1 8.1.1 Predator-Prey Games

Classical Lotka-Volterra models treat predator-prey interactions as deterministic population dynamics. However, incorporating game-theoretic strategies reveals richer phenomena. Consider a system where predators can adopt aggressive or cautious hunting strategies, while prey can choose between vigilant or foraging behaviors.

Definition 8.1 (Predator-Prey Game Matrix). Let the predator-prey interaction be characterized by the payoff matrices:

Predator payoffs: P_p = [[a, b], [c, d]] Prey payoffs: P_r = [[e, f], [g, h]]

where rows represent predator strategies (aggressive, cautious) and columns represent prey strategies (vigilant, foraging).

The evolutionary dynamics couple population changes with strategy evolution. Let x be the fraction of aggressive predators, y the fraction of vigilant prey, N_p the predator population, and N_r the prey population. The system becomes:

dN_p/dt = N_p(rx - δ_p)
dN_r/dt = N_r(r_r - βN_p/N_r)
dx/dt = x(1-x)[π_agg(y) - π_cau(y)]
dy/dt = y(1-y)[π_vig(x) - π_for(x)]

where π_agg, π_cau, π_vig, π_for represent the expected payoffs for each strategy.

using DifferentialEquations, Plots

function predator_prey_game!(du, u, p, t)
    Np, Nr, x, y = u
    r_p, δ_p, r_r, β, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h = p
    
    # Expected payoffs
    π_agg = a * y + b * (1 - y)
    π_cau = c * y + d * (1 - y)
    π_vig = e * x + f * (1 - x)
    π_for = g * x + h * (1 - x)
    
    # Population dynamics
    du[1] = Np * (r_p * x - δ_p)  # Predator population
    du[2] = Nr * (r_r - β * Np / Nr)  # Prey population
    
    # Strategy dynamics
    du[3] = x * (1 - x) * (π_agg - π_cau)  # Fraction of aggressive predators
    du[4] = y * (1 - y) * (π_vig - π_for)  # Fraction of vigilant prey
end

# Parameter values
params = [0.1, 0.05, 0.3, 0.01, -0.5, 0.8, 0.2, 0.3, -0.3, 0.1, 0.6, -0.2]
u0 = [50.0, 100.0, 0.3, 0.7]  # Initial populations and strategies
tspan = (0.0, 100.0)

prob = ODEProblem(predator_prey_game!, u0, params, tspan)
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5())

# Visualization
plot(sol, vars=[1,2], xlabel="Time", ylabel="Population", 
     label=["Predators" "Prey"], linewidth=2)

This coupling generates complex dynamics including strategy oscillations, population cycles, and potential extinction scenarios depending on the payoff structure.

5.1.2 8.1.2 Host-Parasite Coevolution

Host-parasite systems exemplify frequency-dependent selection where each party’s fitness depends on the strategies employed by the other. The Red Queen hypothesis suggests that hosts and parasites engage in perpetual coevolutionary arms races.

Definition 8.2 (Matching Alleles Model). Consider n host genotypes and n parasite genotypes. The fitness of host genotype i against parasite genotype j is:

w_h(i,j) = 1 - c_h if i ≠ j (resistance successful) w_h(i,j) = 1 - s_h if i = j (infection occurs)

Similarly for parasite fitness: w_p(i,j) = b_p - c_p if i = j (successful infection) w_p(i,j) = -c_p if i ≠ j (failed infection)

The replicator dynamics for this system lead to negative frequency-dependent selection, maintaining genetic diversity in both populations.

using LinearAlgebra

function host_parasite_dynamics!(du, u, p, t)
    n = Int(length(u) / 2)
    x = u[1:n]      # Host genotype frequencies
    y = u[n+1:2n]   # Parasite genotype frequencies
    
    c_h, s_h, b_p, c_p = p
    
    # Fitness matrices
    W_h = zeros(n, n)
    W_p = zeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        if i == j
            W_h[i,j] = 1 - s_h
            W_p[i,j] = b_p - c_p
        else
            W_h[i,j] = 1 - c_h
            W_p[i,j] = -c_p
        end
    end
    
    # Mean fitness
    w̄_h = dot(x, W_h * y)
    w̄_p = dot(y, W_p' * x)
    
    # Replicator dynamics
    for i in 1:n
        du[i] = x[i] * (dot(W_h[i,:], y) - w̄_h)
        du[n+i] = y[i] * (dot(W_p[i,:], x) - w̄_p)
    end
end

# Example with 4 genotypes
n = 4
params = [0.1, 0.8, 1.5, 0.2]  # c_h, s_h, b_p, c_p
u0 = vcat(fill(0.25, n), fill(0.25, n))  # Equal initial frequencies

prob = ODEProblem(host_parasite_dynamics!, u0, params, (0.0, 50.0))
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5())

# Visualize genotype frequency oscillations
plot(sol, xlabel="Time", ylabel="Frequency", 
     title="Host-Parasite Coevolutionary Dynamics")

The model predicts sustained oscillations in genotype frequencies, consistent with observed genetic diversity maintenance in natural host-parasite systems.

5.1.3 8.1.3 Niche Construction

Niche construction occurs when organisms modify their environment in ways that affect their own and other species’ fitness. This creates feedback loops between ecological and evolutionary dynamics.

Theorem 8.1 (Niche Construction Feedback). In a system with niche construction, the equilibrium population sizes and strategy frequencies satisfy the coupled system:

F(x, E) = 0 (evolutionary equilibrium) G(x, E) = 0 (ecological equilibrium)

where x* are strategy frequencies, E* is the constructed environment state, F captures frequency-dependent selection, and G describes environmental modification.

Example 8.1: Beaver Dam Construction

Consider beavers that can either build dams (constructors) or not (non-constructors). Dam construction creates wetland habitat that benefits both types but is costly for constructors.

function beaver_niche_construction!(du, u, p, t)
    N, x, E = u  # Population, constructor fraction, habitat quality
    
    r, K, c, b, α, γ = p
    
    # Payoffs
    π_construct = b * E - c
    π_no_construct = b * E
    
    # Population dynamics with environmental feedback
    carrying_capacity = K * (1 + α * E)
    du[1] = r * N * (1 - N / carrying_capacity)
    
    # Strategy dynamics
    du[2] = x * (1 - x) * (π_construct - π_no_construct)
    
    # Environmental dynamics (habitat construction/decay)
    du[3] = γ * x * N - (1 - γ) * E
end

params = [0.1, 100.0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.2, 0.05]
u0 = [50.0, 0.1, 0.1]

prob = ODEProblem(beaver_niche_construction!, u0, params, (0.0, 200.0))
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5())

plot(sol, xlabel="Time", ylabel="Value", 
     label=["Population" "Constructor Fraction" "Habitat Quality"],
     title="Niche Construction Dynamics")

5.2 8.2 Economic Applications

5.2.1 8.2.1 Market Dynamics and Price Formation

Financial markets exhibit complex emergent behaviors arising from the interaction of heterogeneous trading agents. Agent-based models capture phenomena like price bubbles, crashes, and volatility clustering that traditional equilibrium models cannot explain.

Definition 8.3 (Heterogeneous Agent Market Model). Consider a market with N agents, each following strategy i ∈ {1,…,S}. Let n_i(t) be the number of agents using strategy i at time t, and let π_i(t) be the realized profit of strategy i. The market price P(t) evolves according to:

P(t+1) = P(t) + λ ∑_i n_i(t) D_i(P(t), ψ_i)

where D_i is the demand function of strategy i with parameters ψ_i, and λ is a price adjustment parameter.

using Agents, Random, Statistics

@agent Trader AbstractAgent begin
    strategy::Symbol  # :fundamentalist, :chartist, :noise
    wealth::Float64
    position::Float64  # Stock holdings
    memory::Vector{Float64}  # Price history
end

function initialize_market(n_agents::Int)
    space = GraphSpace(complete_graph(1))  # Single market
    model = ABM(Trader, space)
    
    # Add agents with different strategies
    strategies = [:fundamentalist, :chartist, :noise]
    for i in 1:n_agents
        strategy = rand(strategies)
        wealth = 1000.0 + randn() * 100
        add_agent!(model, strategy, wealth, 0.0, Float64[])
    end
    
    # Model properties
    model.price = 100.0
    model.fundamental_value = 100.0
    model.price_history = [100.0]
    model.λ = 0.1  # Price adjustment speed
    
    return model
end

function agent_step!(agent, model)
    price = model.price
    
    if agent.strategy == :fundamentalist
        # Buy if price below fundamental value
        demand = (model.fundamental_value - price) * 0.01
    elseif agent.strategy == :chartist
        # Follow price trends
        if length(model.price_history) >= 2
            trend = model.price_history[end] - model.price_history[end-1]
            demand = trend * 0.05
        else
            demand = 0.0
        end
    else  # :noise
        demand = randn() * 0.1
    end
    
    # Wealth constraint
    max_purchase = agent.wealth / price
    demand = clamp(demand, -agent.position, max_purchase)
    
    # Execute trade
    agent.position += demand
    agent.wealth -= demand * price
    
    return demand
end

function model_step!(model)
    total_demand = sum(agent_step!(agent, model) for agent in allagents(model))
    
    # Update price
    model.price += model.λ * total_demand
    model.price = max(model.price, 1.0)  # Prevent negative prices
    
    push!(model.price_history, model.price)
    
    # Strategy switching based on performance (simplified)
    if length(model.price_history) >= 10
        # Agents occasionally switch to better performing strategies
        for agent in allagents(model)
            if rand() < 0.01  # 1% switch probability
                returns_by_strategy = Dict()
                for strat in [:fundamentalist, :chartist, :noise]
                    returns_by_strategy[strat] = rand()  # Simplified performance metric
                end
                best_strategy = argmax(returns_by_strategy)
                agent.strategy = best_strategy
            end
        end
    end
end

# Run simulation
model = initialize_market(1000)
n_steps = 1000

prices = Float64[]
for _ in 1:n_steps
    step!(model, agent_step!, model_step!)
    push!(prices, model.price)
end

plot(1:n_steps, prices, xlabel="Time", ylabel="Price", 
     title="Agent-Based Market Dynamics", linewidth=2)

This model generates realistic price dynamics including volatility clustering and occasional large price movements, despite the absence of external shocks.

5.2.2 8.2.2 Wealth Inequality: The Yard-Sale Model

The Yard-Sale model demonstrates how wealth inequality can emerge spontaneously from fair exchanges between agents, providing insights into the Pareto distribution of wealth observed in many economies.

Theorem 8.2 (Yard-Sale Convergence). In the Yard-Sale model with conserved wealth W and N agents, if agents trade fractions of their wealth uniformly at random, then the wealth distribution converges to an exponential distribution with a finite number of agents holding most of the wealth.

Proof Sketch: The model conserves total wealth while allowing redistribution. Through a martingale argument, one can show that agents with below-average wealth have a higher probability of losing additional wealth than gaining it, leading to concentration. The stationary distribution emerges from the balance between random walk dynamics and absorbing boundaries at zero wealth.

using Distributions, StatsBase

mutable struct YardSaleAgent
    id::Int
    wealth::Float64
end

function yard_sale_step!(agents, trade_fraction=0.1)
    n = length(agents)
    
    # Random pairwise trading
    for _ in 1:n÷2
        i, j = sample(1:n, 2, replace=false)
        agent1, agent2 = agents[i], agents[j]
        
        if agent1.wealth > 0 && agent2.wealth > 0
            # Each agent risks a fraction of their wealth
            bet1 = min(agent1.wealth * trade_fraction, agent1.wealth)
            bet2 = min(agent2.wealth * trade_fraction, agent2.wealth)
            bet = min(bet1, bet2)
            
            # Fair coin flip
            if rand() < 0.5
                agent1.wealth += bet
                agent2.wealth -= bet
            else
                agent1.wealth -= bet
                agent2.wealth += bet
            end
            
            # Prevent negative wealth
            agent1.wealth = max(0, agent1.wealth)
            agent2.wealth = max(0, agent2.wealth)
        end
    end
end

function simulate_yard_sale(n_agents=1000, n_steps=10000, initial_wealth=100.0)
    agents = [YardSaleAgent(i, initial_wealth) for i in 1:n_agents]
    
    wealth_history = Vector{Vector{Float64}}()
    
    for step in 1:n_steps
        yard_sale_step!(agents)
        
        if step % 500 == 0
            wealths = [agent.wealth for agent in agents]
            push!(wealth_history, copy(wealths))
        end
    end
    
    return agents, wealth_history
end

# Run simulation
agents, wealth_history = simulate_yard_sale()
final_wealths = [agent.wealth for agent in agents]

# Analyze inequality
gini_coefficient = gini(final_wealths)
top_1_percent = quantile(final_wealths, 0.99)
wealth_share_top_1 = sum(w for w in final_wealths if w >= top_1_percent) / sum(final_wealths)

println("Gini coefficient: ", round(gini_coefficient, digits=3))
println("Wealth share of top 1%: ", round(wealth_share_top_1 * 100, digits=1), "%")

# Visualize wealth distribution
histogram(final_wealths, bins=50, xlabel="Wealth", ylabel="Frequency",
          title="Final Wealth Distribution (Yard-Sale Model)", 
          yscale=:log10, alpha=0.7)

The Yard-Sale model reproduces the empirical observation that even with fair exchanges, wealth becomes highly concentrated, with the distribution following approximately exponential or power-law tails.

5.2.3 8.2.3 The Bouchaud-Mézard Model

Building on the Yard-Sale model, Bouchaud and Mézard incorporated saving behavior, which can prevent complete wealth concentration and lead to more realistic inequality levels.

function bouchaud_mezard_step!(agents, λ=0.1, save_rate=0.05)
    n = length(agents)
    
    for _ in 1:n÷2
        i, j = sample(1:n, 2, replace=false)
        agent1, agent2 = agents[i], agents[j]
        
        if agent1.wealth > 0 && agent2.wealth > 0
            # Available wealth for trading (after saving)
            available1 = agent1.wealth * (1 - save_rate)
            available2 = agent2.wealth * (1 - save_rate)
            
            # Trade amount proportional to geometric mean
            trade_amount = λ * sqrt(available1 * available2)
            
            # Fair exchange
            if rand() < 0.5
                transfer = min(trade_amount, available2)
                agent1.wealth += transfer
                agent2.wealth -= transfer
            else
                transfer = min(trade_amount, available1)
                agent1.wealth -= transfer
                agent2.wealth += transfer
            end
            
            # Ensure non-negative wealth
            agent1.wealth = max(0, agent1.wealth)
            agent2.wealth = max(0, agent2.wealth)
        end
    end
end

# Compare models
function compare_inequality_models()
    n_agents = 1000
    n_steps = 5000
    
    # Yard-Sale model
    agents_ys = [YardSaleAgent(i, 100.0) for i in 1:n_agents]
    for _ in 1:n_steps
        yard_sale_step!(agents_ys, 0.1)
    end
    gini_ys = gini([a.wealth for a in agents_ys])
    
    # Bouchaud-Mézard model
    agents_bm = [YardSaleAgent(i, 100.0) for i in 1:n_agents]
    for _ in 1:n_steps
        bouchaud_mezard_step!(agents_bm, 0.1, 0.1)
    end
    gini_bm = gini([a.wealth for a in agents_bm])
    
    println("Yard-Sale Gini: ", round(gini_ys, digits=3))
    println("Bouchaud-Mézard Gini: ", round(gini_bm, digits=3))
    
    return agents_ys, agents_bm
end

compare_inequality_models()

The introduction of saving behavior in the Bouchaud-Mézard model leads to more moderate inequality levels, suggesting policy interventions that encourage saving can mitigate extreme wealth concentration.

5.3 8.3 Social Applications

5.3.1 8.3.1 Norm Emergence

Social norms emerge through repeated interactions where individuals learn to coordinate their behavior. The emergence process can be modeled as evolutionary game dynamics on social networks.

Definition 8.4 (Coordination Game with Multiple Equilibria). A coordination game has payoff matrix:

    A    B
A  [a,a] [0,0]
B  [0,0] [b,b]

When played on a network, agents update strategies based on local interactions, leading to the emergence of coordinated regions.

using Graphs, Random

@agent SocialAgent AbstractAgent begin
    strategy::Symbol  # :A or :B
    payoff::Float64
    update_prob::Float64
end

function initialize_norm_model(network, n_agents)
    model = ABM(SocialAgent, GraphSpace(network))
    
    # Add agents to each node
    for i in 1:n_agents
        strategy = rand([:A, :B])
        add_agent!(i, model, strategy, 0.0, 0.1)
    end
    
    # Game parameters
    model.payoff_A = 2.0
    model.payoff_B = 1.0
    model.noise = 0.01
    
    return model
end

function agent_step!(agent, model)
    neighbors = nearby_agents(agent, model)
    
    if length(neighbors) == 0
        return
    end
    
    # Calculate expected payoffs for both strategies
    neighbor_strategies = [neighbor.strategy for neighbor in neighbors]
    
    payoff_A = count(s -> s == :A, neighbor_strategies) * model.payoff_A
    payoff_B = count(s -> s == :B, neighbor_strategies) * model.payoff_B
    
    agent.payoff = agent.strategy == :A ? payoff_A : payoff_B
    
    # Best response with noise
    if rand() < agent.update_prob
        if payoff_A > payoff_B + randn() * model.noise
            agent.strategy = :A
        elseif payoff_B > payoff_A + randn() * model.noise
            agent.strategy = :B
        end
    end
end

function measure_consensus(model)
    strategies = [agent.strategy for agent in allagents(model)]
    n_A = count(s -> s == :A, strategies)
    return max(n_A, length(strategies) - n_A) / length(strategies)
end

# Simulate norm emergence on different network topologies
function simulate_norm_emergence(network_type="small_world")
    if network_type == "small_world"
        network = watts_strogatz(100, 6, 0.1)
    elseif network_type == "scale_free"
        network = barabasi_albert(100, 3)
    else
        network = erdos_renyi(100, 0.1)
    end
    
    model = initialize_norm_model(network, 100)
    
    consensus_over_time = Float64[]
    for _ in 1:200
        step!(model, agent_step!)
        push!(consensus_over_time, measure_consensus(model))
    end
    
    return consensus_over_time
end

# Compare convergence on different networks
networks = ["small_world", "scale_free", "random"]
results = Dict()

for net in networks
    results[net] = simulate_norm_emergence(net)
end

plot([results[net] for net in networks], 
     label=networks, xlabel="Time", ylabel="Consensus Level",
     title="Norm Emergence on Different Network Topologies")

The model shows that network structure significantly affects the speed and likelihood of norm emergence, with small-world networks often facilitating faster consensus than random or scale-free networks.

5.3.2 8.3.2 Institution Formation

Institutions arise to solve collective action problems. We model this as the evolution of cooperation in public goods games with the possibility of institutional enforcement.

Definition 8.5 (Public Goods Game with Institutions). In a public goods game with institutions, players can choose to: 1. Contribute to public good (C) 2. Free-ride (D)
3. Support institutional enforcement (I)

The institution imposes costs on free-riders but requires collective support to maintain.

@agent InstitutionalAgent AbstractAgent begin
    strategy::Symbol  # :cooperate, :defect, :institution
    wealth::Float64
    reputation::Float64
end

function initialize_institution_model(n_agents)
    space = GraphSpace(complete_graph(n_agents))
    model = ABM(InstitutionalAgent, space)
    
    for i in 1:n_agents
        strategy = rand([:cooperate, :defect, :institution])
        add_agent!(model, strategy, 100.0, 0.5)
    end
    
    # Game parameters
    model.public_good_multiplier = 2.0
    model.contribution_cost = 1.0
    model.institution_cost = 2.0
    model.enforcement_fine = 3.0
    model.institution_threshold = 0.3
    
    return model
end

function play_public_goods_with_institutions!(model)
    agents = collect(allagents(model))
    n = length(agents)
    
    # Count strategies
    n_coop = count(a -> a.strategy == :cooperate, agents)
    n_inst = count(a -> a.strategy == :institution, agents)
    n_defect = count(a -> a.strategy == :defect, agents)
    
    # Check if institution is active
    institution_active = (n_inst / n) >= model.institution_threshold
    
    # Calculate public good
    total_contributions = n_coop + n_inst
    public_good_benefit = (total_contributions * model.public_good_multiplier) / n
    
    # Distribute payoffs
    for agent in agents
        if agent.strategy == :cooperate
            agent.wealth += public_good_benefit - model.contribution_cost
        elseif agent.strategy == :defect
            agent.wealth += public_good_benefit
            # Apply fine if institution is active
            if institution_active
                agent.wealth -= model.enforcement_fine
                agent.reputation *= 0.9  # Reputation damage
            end
        else  # :institution
            agent.wealth += public_good_benefit - model.contribution_cost - model.institution_cost
        end
    end
end

function agent_step!(agent, model)
    # Strategy updating based on success
    if rand() < 0.1  # 10% update probability
        # Sample another agent and compare
        other = rand(allagents(model))
        
        if other.wealth > agent.wealth
            # Imitate successful strategy with some noise
            if rand() < 0.8
                agent.strategy = other.strategy
            else
                agent.strategy = rand([:cooperate, :defect, :institution])
            end
        end
    end
end

function model_step!(model)
    play_public_goods_with_institutions!(model)
end

# Simulate institutional evolution
model = initialize_institution_model(100)

strategy_counts = Dict(:cooperate => Int[], :defect => Int[], :institution => Int[])
average_wealth = Float64[]

for step in 1:500
    step!(model, agent_step!, model_step!)
    
    agents = collect(allagents(model))
    for strategy in [:cooperate, :defect, :institution]
        count = sum(1 for a in agents if a.strategy == strategy)
        push!(strategy_counts[strategy], count)
    end
    push!(average_wealth, mean(a.wealth for a in agents))
end

# Visualize evolution of strategies
plot([strategy_counts[:cooperate], strategy_counts[:defect], strategy_counts[:institution]],
     label=["Cooperators" "Defectors" "Institution Supporters"],
     xlabel="Time", ylabel="Number of Agents", 
     title="Evolution of Institutional Support")

5.3.3 8.3.3 Revolution Dynamics

Revolutionary movements can be modeled as threshold phenomena where individual participation decisions depend on the expected participation of others, leading to sudden regime changes.

Theorem 8.3 (Revolutionary Threshold Model). In a population where agent i participates in revolution if the expected participation rate exceeds their threshold θ_i, there exist multiple equilibria. Small changes in the threshold distribution can lead to discontinuous jumps between stable and unstable regimes.

@agent Revolutionary AbstractAgent begin
    threshold::Float64
    participating::Bool
    grievance::Float64
    risk_tolerance::Float64
end

function initialize_revolution_model(n_agents)
    space = GraphSpace(watts_strogatz(n_agents, 8, 0.1))
    model = ABM(Revolutionary, space)
    
    for i in 1:n_agents
        threshold = rand(Beta(2, 2))  # Threshold for participation
        grievance = rand(Exponential(1))
        risk_tolerance = rand()
        add_agent!(i, model, threshold, false, grievance, risk_tolerance)
    end
    
    model.regime_strength = 0.8
    model.success_probability = 0.0
    
    return model
end

function agent_step!(agent, model)
    # Observe local participation rate
    neighbors = collect(nearby_agents(agent, model))
    local_participation = isempty(neighbors) ? 0.0 : 
        mean(neighbor.participating for neighbor in neighbors)
    
    # Global information with noise
    global_participation = mean(a.participating for a in allagents(model))
    perceived_participation = 0.7 * global_participation + 0.3 * local_participation + 0.1 * randn()
    perceived_participation = clamp(perceived_participation, 0, 1)
    
    # Participation decision
    expected_benefit = agent.grievance * perceived_participation
    expected_cost = (1 - perceived_participation) * model.regime_strength * (1 - agent.risk_tolerance)
    
    agent.participating = (expected_benefit > expected_cost) && (perceived_participation > agent.threshold)
end

function model_step!(model)
    participation_rate = mean(a.participating for a in allagents(model))
    
    # Revolution succeeds if participation exceeds critical threshold
    if participation_rate > 0.5
        model.regime_strength *= 0.95  # Regime weakens
        model.success_probability = participation_rate
    else
        model.regime_strength = min(1.0, model.regime_strength * 1.01)  # Regime recovers
        model.success_probability = 0.0
    end
end

# Simulate revolution dynamics
model = initialize_revolution_model(1000)

participation_rates = Float64[]
regime_strengths = Float64[]

for _ in 1:200
    step!(model, agent_step!, model_step!)
    push!(participation_rates, mean(a.participating for a in allagents(model)))
    push!(regime_strengths, model.regime_strength)
end

plot([participation_rates, regime_strengths],
     label=["Participation Rate" "Regime Strength"],
     xlabel="Time", ylabel="Probability", 
     title="Revolutionary Dynamics")

5.4 8.4 Computational Experiments and Analysis

5.4.1 8.4.1 Parameter Sweeps and Phase Diagrams

Understanding the parameter dependence of agent-based models requires systematic exploration of parameter space. Phase diagrams reveal regions of qualitatively different behavior.

```julia using Plots, ProgressMeter

function parameter_sweep_predator_prey(param_ranges, n_trials=5) results = Dict()

r_p_range, β_range = param_ranges

@showprogress for r_p in r_p_range, β in β_range
    outcomes = []
    
    for trial in 1:n_trials
        params = [r_p, 0.05, 0.3, β, -0.5, 0.8, 0.2, 0.3, -0.3, 0.1, 0.6, -0.2]
        u0 = [50.0, 100.0, 0.3, 0.7] .+ 0.1 .* randn
